posts.title: agents in production vs agents in demos
posts.hook: Most AI agent demos run on vibes. Most AI agent deployments run on retries, fallbacks, and ugly if-statements.
posts.body:
Most AI agent demos run on vibes. Most AI agent deployments run on retries, fallbacks, and ugly if-statements.

I've been running Claude Code agents in production for months now. Daily crons, content pipelines, data enrichment, CRM syncs. Here's the pattern nobody shows in the demo video:

80% of your agent code is error handling.

The LLM call itself is maybe 15 lines. The retry logic, the output validation, the fallback when the model hallucinates a field name, the logging so you can debug at 2am when the cron fails... that's the actual work.

Teams shipping agents that work are not the ones with the fanciest frameworks. They're the ones who treat agents like infrastructure, not magic.

That means:

✅ Structured outputs with validation
✅ Deterministic fallbacks when the model gets weird
✅ Logging everything, not just errors
✅ Running the boring version first, then adding autonomy

The gap between "agent demo" and "agent in production" is the same gap that's always existed between prototype and product. The teams closing that gap fastest are the ones who already knew how to ship reliable software.

AI didn't change the engineering. It changed what the engineering builds.
posts.cta: What's been your ugliest agent failure in production? The ones that teach you the most are always the ones that break at 3am.

remixes.label: iceberg-architecture
remixes.hook: The LLM call in your agent is 15 lines. The code keeping it alive is 500.
remixes.body:
The LLM call in your agent is 15 lines. The code keeping it alive is 500.

I call this the Agent Iceberg. What you see in the demo: a clean prompt, a beautiful response, maybe a tool call that works on the first try.

What's underneath: retry logic with exponential backoff. Output validation that catches when the model returns markdown instead of JSON. Fallback chains for when your primary model is rate-limited. State management so a failed step doesn't corrupt everything downstream. Logging so you can figure out why Tuesday's cron run produced garbage.

I run about a dozen agent pipelines daily. Content generation, CRM syncing, data enrichment, Reddit monitoring. Every single one has more error handling code than business logic.

 is that production agents aren't elegant. They're duct tape wrapped around a probabilistic system. The skill isn't writing the prompt. It's writing the 80% of code that catches the prompt when it falls.

Demo culture has people thinking agents are plug-and-play. They're not. They're infrastructure projects disguised as API calls.
remixes.cta: If your agent doesn't have a retry strategy, a validation layer, and structured logging, it's not production-ready. It's a demo with a cron job.

remixes.label: 3am-postmortem
remixes.hook: My agent pipeline broke at 3am last Tuesday. The fix taught me more than any AI conference talk.
remixes.body:
My agent pipeline broke at 3am last Tuesday. The fix taught me more than any AI conference talk.

The content pipeline ran fine for weeks. Then one night the model returned a response with a trailing comma in the JSON. One character. It cascaded through three downstream systems.

Here's what demo videos never prepare you for. Models are non-deterministic. What worked 10,000 times will fail on attempt 10,001 in a way you didn't think to handle. Your agent needs to be more paranoid than you are.

Since that incident I've adopted a rule: every agent output gets validated against a schema before it touches anything downstream. Every pipeline step is idempotent so reruns don't create duplicates. Every failure gets logged with enough context to debug without reproducing.

This isn't glamorous work. Nobody's posting about their Zod validation schemas or their dead letter queues on LinkedIn. But this is what separates agents that run for months from agents that run for a demo.

The real skill in AI engineering isn't prompting. It's building systems that survive the model's worst day.
remixes.cta: What's the dumbest failure mode you've hit with an AI system in production? I'm collecting war stories.

If you're deploying AI agents beyond demos, here's what I'd recommend based on running them in production daily for months.

The LLM call itself is roughly 15 lines of code. The surrounding infrastructure is 10x that. You need retry logic with exponential backoff. Output validation against strict schemas. Fallback chains for rate limits and model degradation. Idempotent pipeline steps so failures don't cascade. Structured logging with enough context to debug at 3am without guessing.

The organizations succeeding with agents aren't the ones with the best prompts. They're the ones who treat agents as distributed systems problems, not magic API calls.
tones.provocateur:
The entire AI agent ecosystem is built on a lie. The lie is that the hard part is the prompt.

Watch any agent demo. Clean input, perfect output, audience claps. Now go run that same agent 1,000 times against real-world data. Watch it choke on edge cases the demo creator never considered.

I've been running agents in production for months. Daily. Unattended. The honest breakdown: 15 lines of LLM calls, 500 lines of code catching the LLM when it inevitably produces garbage.

Retry logic. Schema validation. Fallback chains. Dead letter queues. Idempotent operations. Structured logging. This is agent engineering. Not the clean prompt you saw on Twitter.

80% of production agent code is error handling. If that number surprises you, you haven't shipped one yet.

The agent hype cycle has people thinking they're one prompt away from automation. They're actually one cascading failure away from learning what production actually means. The models are impressive. The infrastructure required to make them reliable is boring, ugly, and absolutely non-negotiable.

posts.title: GTM engineering is not revops with a new title
posts.hook: RevOps optimizes the machine. GTM Engineering builds a different machine entirely.
posts.body:
RevOps optimizes the machine. GTM Engineering builds a different machine entirely.

I keep seeing job posts for "GTM Engineer" that are just RevOps with a salary bump. Configure Salesforce flows. Maintain HubSpot integrations. Build dashboards.

That's not it.

GTM Engineering means you're writing code that generates pipeline. You're building enrichment waterfalls in Clay that score accounts before a human ever sees them. You're deploying AI agents that research companies and draft personalized sequences. You're connecting signals from 6 different tools into one unified buying intent score.

The difference is who you report to and what you ship.

RevOps ships reports. GTM Engineering ships pipeline.

RevOps answers "what happened." GTM Engineering answers "what should we do next" and then does it automatically.

The best GTM engineers I know came from one of two backgrounds: SDRs who learned to code, or engineers who learned how revenue actually works. Both paths share the same thing. They've felt the pain of manual outbound and decided to automate themselves out of it.

If your "GTM Engineer" doesn't have API keys to your enrichment stack and a local dev environment, you hired a RevOps analyst.
posts.cta: Building a GTM engineering function? The first hire matters more than the stack. Get someone who's done the manual work and hated it enough to automate it.

inspired_by.summary: Discusses building GTM engineering teams by integrating outbound automation tools with custom AI workflows. Emphasizes the need for sales ops to own data pipelines for scalable lead gen. Shares a framework for revops to measure automation ROI.

remixes.label: builder-vs-optimizer
remixes.hook: RevOps asks 'how do we get 5% more out of this funnel?' GTM Engineering asks 'what if we built a completely different funnel?'
remixes.body:
RevOps asks 'how do we get 5% more out of this funnel?' GTM Engineering asks 'what if we built a completely different funnel?'

This distinction matters because it changes who you hire, what tools you buy, and what outcomes you can expect.

RevOps is optimization. Improving conversion rates, cleaning data, building reports, maintaining integrations. Important work. Not engineering.

GTM Engineering is building systems that create pipeline from scratch. Writing enrichment waterfalls that score and route accounts automatically. Building intent signal pipelines that surface buying behavior before the SDR even opens their laptop. Creating automation that turns raw data into sequenced, personalized outbound at scale.

The difference shows up in the interview. A RevOps hire talks about Salesforce admin certs and dashboard templates. A GTM Engineer talks about the Python script they wrote to cross-reference intent data with firmographic signals, then auto-enroll matched accounts into personalized sequences.

One maintains the machine. The other builds machines that didn't exist before.

If your 'GTM Engineer' job post lists Salesforce configuration as a primary skill, you're hiring RevOps at a premium. That's fine if that's what you need. Just call it what it is.
remixes.cta: The test is simple: does this person build new systems, or optimize existing ones? Both are valuable. Only one is engineering.

remixes.label: job-post-autopsy
remixes.hook: I reviewed 50 'GTM Engineer' job posts this month. 42 of them were RevOps jobs with inflated titles.
remixes.body:
I reviewed 50 'GTM Engineer' job posts this month. 42 of them were RevOps jobs with inflated titles.

The tells are obvious once you know what to look for.

RevOps with a new title: 'Maintain HubSpot workflows. Configure Salesforce integrations. Build executive dashboards. Manage lead routing rules.'

Actual GTM Engineering: 'Build enrichment pipelines that score accounts from raw intent signals. Write code that generates pipeline. Design and deploy automation that replaces manual prospecting workflows.'

The first role keeps the existing system running. The second role builds new systems that create revenue.

This matters because the skills are different. RevOps is admin work, process optimization, and analytics. GTM Engineering is software development applied to go-to-market. You need someone who can write Python, build API integrations, architect data pipelines, and think about systems design.

When you hire a RevOps person for a GTM Engineering role, you get better dashboards. When you hire a GTM Engineer, you get net-new pipeline that didn't exist before the hire.

The salary premium for 'GTM Engineer' only makes sense if the person is actually engineering. Building, not configuring.
remixes.cta: Before you post that GTM Engineer role, ask: does this person need to write code? If not, you're hiring RevOps. Nothing wrong with that. Just pay accordingly.

tones.advisor:
Here's a framework I use to distinguish GTM Engineering from RevOps, because the confusion is costing companies real money on misaligned hires.

RevOps optimizes existing systems. They configure Salesforce, maintain HubSpot workflows, build dashboards, and improve conversion rates within the current architecture. This is essential work.

GTM Engineering builds new systems. They write enrichment pipelines that score accounts from raw data. They build intent signal infrastructure that surfaces buying behavior automatically. They create automation that turns manual prospecting into programmatic pipeline generation.

My recommendation: look at the core competency required. If the role needs someone who configures tools, that's RevOps. If it needs someone who writes code that generates pipeline, that's GTM Engineering.

The practical test is straightforward. Ask candidates to describe something they built from scratch that directly created pipeline. RevOps candidates will describe optimizing an existing process. GTM Engineers will describe systems they coded that didn't exist before.

Get this distinction right and you'll hire the right person at the right compensation. Get it wrong and you'll pay engineering salaries for admin work, or worse, expect admin hires to do engineering.
tones.provocateur:
Let's be honest. Most companies posting 'GTM Engineer' roles have no idea what GTM Engineering actually is. They just heard the title converts better on LinkedIn.

They want someone to fix their broken HubSpot workflows and maybe write a Zapier automation or two. That's RevOps. It's been RevOps for a decade. Putting a new title on it doesn't change the work.

Real GTM Engineering means writing code that generates revenue. Not configuring SaaS tools. Not building dashboards. Writing actual software. Enrichment waterfalls, intent signal pipelines, automated scoring systems, programmatic outbound infrastructure.

The industry inflated this title because RevOps salaries plateaued and companies needed a way to attract technical talent without admitting they don't know what to do with them.

Here's the damage: actual GTM Engineers now have to wade through 50 RevOps job posts to find the 8 that involve real engineering. And companies wonder why the role is hard to fill.

You want to hire a GTM Engineer? The job description should mention Python, APIs, data pipelines, and system architecture. If it mentions Salesforce admin certification, you're looking for a different person.

posts.title: shipping a product every week with AI
posts.hook: I shipped more working software in the last 30 days than I did in all of 2024.
posts.body:
I shipped more working software in the last 30 days than I did in all of 2024.

Not prototypes. Not demos. Production systems running on crons, serving real users, generating real data.

A daily autonomous blog generator. An AI chat widget on three different sites. A full CRM integration pipeline. A content distribution system. A Reddit engagement tool. A landing page generator.

All built by one person on a Mac Mini.

The unlock wasn't learning a new framework or finding a co-founder. It was treating AI coding tools as a genuine collaborator instead of a fancy autocomplete.

Claude Code with a well-structured codebase and clear instructions doesn't just write code faster. It changes what's even possible for a solo builder. I architect systems I never would have attempted alone. Not because I couldn't figure them out, but because the calendar math never worked.

Pieter Levels proved solo founders could build million-dollar products. What's different now is the velocity floor. The minimum viable shipping speed for one person with AI tools is 5-10x what it was two years ago.

The constraint isn't technical ability anymore. It's taste, prioritization, and knowing what to build next.
posts.cta: Solo builders with AI tools are quietly outshipping 5-person teams. The gap is only getting wider.

remixes.label: output-inventory
remixes.hook: Here's every production system I shipped in the last 30 days. One person. No team. No funding round.
remixes.body:
Here's every production system I shipped in the last 30 days. One person. No team. No funding round.

A daily autonomous blog generator that writes and publishes without intervention. An AI chat widget running on three different production websites. A full CRM integration pipeline syncing enriched data across systems. A content distribution engine that takes one piece and adapts it across platforms. A Reddit engagement tool that scouts and surfaces opportunities. A landing page generator that researches companies and builds custom pages.

Each one runs on crons, serves real users, and generates real data. Not prototypes sitting in a repo.

The enabler is obvious: AI coding tools changed the math on what one person can build. But the tools alone aren't the insight. Plenty of people have access to Claude Code and Cursor. The difference is treating these tools as force multipliers for actual shipping, not for building demos.

My approach: I build the simplest version that works, deploy it immediately, then iterate based on real usage data. No spec documents. No architecture reviews. No waiting for approval from a team that doesn't exist.

The individual builder era isn't theoretical anymore. It's running in production.
remixes.cta: The overhead of coordination is real. Cut it, and one focused builder with AI tools ships faster than most small teams. That's not a hot take. It's just math.

remixes.label: team-replacement-math
remixes.hook: A five-person startup burns $80K/month minimum. I'm shipping comparable output for the cost of an API subscription.
remixes.body:
A five-person startup burns $80K/month minimum. I'm shipping comparable output for the cost of an API subscription.

I don't say this to be provocative. I say it because the economics of building software have fundamentally changed and most people haven't updated their mental models.

In the old world, you needed a frontend developer, a backend developer, a DevOps person, maybe a designer, and a PM to coordinate them. Five salaries. Five calendars. Five opinions on architecture. Meetings about meetings.

In my world right now: I describe what I need to an AI coding assistant. It writes the code. I review, adjust, deploy. The feedback loop is minutes, not sprints.

Last month I shipped six production systems. Blog generator, chat widgets, CRM pipeline, content distribution, Reddit engagement tool, landing page builder. All running, all serving users.

The quality gap that used to require specialists is closing fast. AI handles the boilerplate. I handle the architecture decisions and domain knowledge. The combination moves faster than a coordinated team because there's zero coordination overhead.

This isn't about AI replacing developers. It's about one developer with AI tools having the throughput that used to require a team.
remixes.cta: If you're a solo builder and you're not leveraging AI tools to multiply your output, you're competing with people who are. The math doesn't lie.

Meanwhile, the funded startup down the street spent the same 30 days in sprint planning, architecture debates, and Notion documentation. They shipped a revised onboarding flow.

The dirty secret nobody in VC wants to acknowledge: coordination overhead is the actual bottleneck in most startups. Not talent, not funding, not market timing. Meetings. Alignment. Communication. That overhead scales quadratically with team size.

Remove it entirely and one focused person with AI tools operates at a velocity that makes five-person teams look bureaucratic. Because they are.

I'm not saying teams are obsolete. I'm saying the threshold for when you need a team just moved dramatically upward. If you're hiring your third engineer before your product has traction, you're optimizing for comfort, not output.

The era of the solo builder isn't coming. It arrived while everyone was writing hiring plans.

posts.title: cursor and devin miss the point
posts.hook: Everyone's arguing about whether Cursor or Devin writes better code. They're asking the wrong question.
posts.body:
Everyone's arguing about whether Cursor or Devin writes better code. They're asking the wrong question.

The question isn't which AI tool writes the best code. It's which one understands your codebase well enough to make the right architectural decisions.

I've tried most of them. Here's what actually matters in practice:

1. Context window and how it's used. Can the tool hold your entire project structure in its head? Or does it forget the API route you showed it 5 minutes ago?

2. Instruction following. Can you give it project-specific rules and trust it won't violate them? My CLAUDE.md is 200+ lines of constraints. That's not overhead. That's what makes the output production-ready.

3. Tool access. Can it run your tests, check your build, read your logs? A code generator that can't verify its own output is a suggestion engine, not a collaborator.

The AI code tool that wins long-term isn't the one that writes the cleverest function. It's the one that understands the system it's building inside of.

Benchmarks test isolated coding puzzles. Production tests whether the tool can maintain a growing codebase without introducing regressions.

Those are completely different skills.
posts.cta: What's your actual evaluation criteria when picking AI dev tools? Curious what matters most to people shipping real products.

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Highlights trending developer tools integrating AI agents for code automation, such as Cursor and Devin. Shares case studies on productivity gains in software teams using agentic workflows. Calls for more robust APIs to enable seamless automation in dev pipelines.

remixes.label: context-is-king
remixes.hook: I stopped evaluating AI coding tools on code quality. The only metric that matters is context comprehension.
remixes.body:
I stopped evaluating AI coding tools on code quality. The only metric that matters is context comprehension.

Every AI coding tool can write a clean function. That's table stakes. The question that actually determines whether a tool saves you time or creates tech debt is: does it understand your codebase deeply enough to make decisions you'd agree with?

I work in a monorepo with three Next.js apps, shared packages, Python scripts, and Swift projects. Any tool that only sees the current file is useless to me. I need a tool that understands my shared component library when I'm editing a page. That knows my database schema when I'm writing an API route. That respects my project's conventions without being told every time.

After months of real usage, here's what I've found actually matters. First, context window size and how intelligently it's used. Can the tool pull in relevant files automatically? Second, codebase awareness. Does it understand project structure and conventions? Third, persistence across sessions. Does it remember what we discussed yesterday?

Code generation quality is nearly identical across the top tools. The differentiation is entirely in how well they understand the world around the code they're writing.

Stop benchmarking on isolated coding challenges. Start evaluating on real projects with real complexity.
remixes.cta: Next time you're evaluating an AI dev tool, give it a task that requires understanding three files in different directories. That's where the real differences show up.

remixes.label: wrong-question-framework
remixes.hook: The AI coding tool debate is Vim vs Emacs all over again. Loud, tribal, and missing the actual point.
remixes.body:
The AI coding tool debate is Vim vs Emacs all over again. Loud, tribal, and missing the actual point.

Cursor fans dunk on Copilot. Devin advocates argue it's the future. Claude Code users (myself included) think everyone else is missing the context window advantage. None of this helps you ship software.

Here's the framework I actually use to evaluate these tools after months of daily production use.

Does it understand my project structure without hand-holding? I work across a monorepo with multiple apps and shared packages. If I have to manually point the tool at every relevant file, I've lost half the productivity gain.

Does it maintain context across sessions? The most expensive part of using AI tools is re-explaining your codebase every morning. Tools that persist project understanding across conversations save hours per week.

Does it make architectural decisions I agree with? Not just syntactically correct code, but code that fits my patterns, respects my conventions, and integrates cleanly with existing systems.

Does it handle real-world complexity? Meaning multiple languages, build systems, deployment targets, and dependencies. Not a single-file Python script.

The tool that scores highest on these four questions is the one that ships the most production code. Everything else is tribalism.
remixes.cta: Evaluate AI dev tools on your actual codebase, not on LeetCode problems. The results will surprise you.

Benchmarks test isolated coding puzzles. Production tests whether the tool can maintain a growing codebase without introducing regressions.

Those are completely different skills.

posts.title: outbound automation destroyed our deliverability
posts.hook: We automated outbound to 3,000 contacts a week and our domain reputation cratered within 14 days.
posts.body:
We automated outbound to 3,000 contacts a week and our domain reputation cratered within 14 days.

This was years ago. Before I knew what I know now. We had shiny new automation, enriched data, and AI-written sequences. The math looked beautiful on paper.

What we didn't have: proper warmup, domain rotation, send throttling, or any concept of deliverability infrastructure.

The emails were great. Nobody saw them. They went straight to spam.

Here's what I learned rebuilding from that:

🔧 Infrastructure before automation. Warm domains for 4-6 weeks minimum. Set up DKIM, DMARC, SPF on every sending domain. Use dedicated sending domains, never your primary.

🔧 Volume is not a strategy. 200 highly targeted emails from a warm domain outperform 3,000 spray-and-pray messages every single time. I know because I've run both.

🔧 Enrichment quality determines everything downstream. If your Clay waterfall is pulling bad emails, your automation just accelerates the damage.

The teams winning at outbound right now aren't the ones sending the most. They're the ones with the cleanest data, the warmest infrastructure, and the most specific targeting.

Automation multiplies whatever you feed it. Feed it garbage, you get garbage at scale.
posts.cta: If you're scaling outbound and haven't audited your deliverability infrastructure first, stop and do that today. Seriously.

inspired_by.author: Dan Tyre
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down revops best practices for integrating sales ops with outbound tools. Focuses on automation pitfalls and content-led GTM strategies. Shares tactical advice for engineering efficient revenue flywheels.

remixes.label: infrastructure-first
remixes.hook: Your emails could be perfect and it wouldn't matter. Without deliverability infrastructure, they're landing in spam.
remixes.body:
Your emails could be perfect and it wouldn't matter. Without deliverability infrastructure, they're landing in spam.

I learned this the expensive way. Years ago we had everything that looked right: enriched data, AI-written sequences, automated workflows hitting 3,000 contacts per week. Open rates looked solid for the first few days.

Then the domain reputation scores started dropping. Then Gmail started routing us to spam. Then Outlook followed. Within two weeks we'd burned a domain that took months to warm up.

The problem wasn't the content or the targeting. The problem was we treated deliverability as an afterthought when it should have been the foundation.

Here's what we were missing. Proper domain warmup with a gradual volume ramp over weeks, not days. Domain rotation across multiple sending domains so no single domain takes the full volume hit. Send throttling that respects provider-specific limits. SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configured correctly. Bounce and complaint monitoring with automatic suppression.

This is plumbing. It's boring. Nobody wants to spend their first two weeks of outbound setup on DNS records and warmup schedules. But skip it and the best email copy in the world ends up in a spam folder nobody checks.
remixes.cta: Before you write a single outbound email, audit your sending infrastructure. Warmup, rotation, authentication, throttling. Get the plumbing right first.

remixes.label: automation-trap
remixes.hook: Outbound automation doesn't scale your pipeline. It scales your mistakes.
remixes.body:
Outbound automation doesn't scale your pipeline. It scales your mistakes.

Every outbound team hits the same inflection point. Manual outreach works. Results come in. Someone says 'let's automate and 10x this.' So you buy the tools, enrich the data, write the sequences, and crank the volume to 3,000 contacts per week.

Two weeks later your domain reputation is destroyed and you're starting over.

I've lived this. The seductive math of automation hides a critical assumption: that your process is ready to scale. If you're manually sending 50 emails a day and getting responses, that doesn't mean automating 500 a day will get 10x responses. It means automating 500 a day will 10x whatever's broken in your process.

Bad data at 50 sends? Annoying. Bad data at 3,000? Domain blacklisted.

No warmup protocol at 50 sends? Doesn't matter. No warmup protocol at 3,000? Deliverability crater.

Skipping suppression lists at 50 sends? Minor risk. At 3,000? Spam complaints spike and you're flagged across providers.

Automation is a multiplier. If you multiply a broken process, you get a bigger broken process. Faster.

The right sequence: fix the process manually, verify every assumption at low volume, then and only then automate.
remixes.cta: The best outbound teams I've seen all did the same thing: they got manual outreach working perfectly before automating anything. Boring, but it works.

If you're planning to scale outbound, here's the infrastructure checklist I recommend before you send a single automated email.

I learned this from making the exact mistake you're about to make. We automated outbound to 3,000 contacts weekly and our domain reputation cratered in 14 days. The emails were well-written. The targeting was solid. None of it mattered because the infrastructure wasn't ready.

Here's what I'd recommend you build first. Domain warmup: start with low volume and ramp gradually over 2-4 weeks. Don't skip this. Domain rotation: use multiple sending domains so no single domain absorbs the full volume. Send throttling: respect per-provider limits. Gmail, Outlook, and Yahoo all have different thresholds. Authentication: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configured and verified on every sending domain. Monitoring: track bounce rates, complaint rates, and domain reputation scores daily. Set up automatic suppression when thresholds are crossed.

This is a 2-3 week setup before you send anything at scale. I know that feels slow. But rebuilding a burned domain takes months. The math favors patience.

The teams that get outbound right treat deliverability as infrastructure, not an afterthought. Build the foundation before you build the volume.

Most outbound automation isn't a growth strategy. It's an accelerated way to burn your domain and blame the tool.

We did exactly this. 3,000 contacts a week. Shiny enrichment. AI-written sequences. Domain reputation cratered in two weeks flat. Know whose fault it was? Ours. Entirely.

The outbound tool vendors won't tell you this because it's bad for sales, but automation without deliverability infrastructure is just spam at scale. That's it. You're not doing 'outbound.' You're spamming faster.

No warmup protocol. No domain rotation. No send throttling. No bounce monitoring. But hey, the sequences were personalized, so surely that matters, right? It doesn't. Gmail doesn't care how good your copy is if your domain health score is in the gutter.

The entire outbound automation industry sells you the engine without mentioning you need roads. Then when your deliverability craters, they sell you a 'deliverability add-on' that should have been step one.

Here's : if you can't get results sending 50 manual emails a day, automating 3,000 a week won't fix your problem. It'll just make the failure louder and more expensive.

Fix your infrastructure. Then automate. Not the other way around.

posts.title: the career pivot nobody talks about
posts.hook: I didn't quit my job to start a company. I got laid off and decided not to go back.
posts.body:
I didn't quit my job to start a company. I got laid off and decided not to go back.

There's this narrative on LinkedIn where every founder has a clean origin story. "I saw an opportunity and I seized it." "I couldn't ignore my calling anymore."

My version: the company I helped build went through changes, I found myself on the other side, and I had to make a choice. Go find another VP of Sales Ops role, or take everything I'd built internally and offer it to the market.

The pivot from operator to builder isn't glamorous. The first few months are terrifying. No steady paycheck. No team to delegate to. No IT department setting up your tools.

But here's what I had that most people starting from zero don't: years of building production systems. Real GTM pipelines that generated real pipeline. A deep understanding of what companies actually need vs. what vendors sell them.

The gap between "I could build this" and "I am building this" is just deciding that this week's problem is worth solving with your own hands instead of someone else's budget.

Six months in. Building every day. Shipping things that would have taken a team of four at my last company.
posts.cta: If you're on the fence about going independent, the best time is when you have a skill stack that solves expensive problems. The second best time is when you have no choice.

inspired_by.author: Sahil Lavingia
inspired_by.summary: Sahil recounts pivoting from Pinterest to starting Gumroad solo, growing it to millions in revenue while building in public. He discusses embracing failure, minimal viable products, and creator economy trends. His transparency has built a massive following of builders.

remixes.label: myth-busting-founder-origin
remixes.hook: Most founder origin stories are retroactive fiction. Mine included.
remixes.body:
Most founder origin stories are retroactive fiction. Mine included.

We love the narrative arc. Visionary sees gap in market. Visionary quits cushy job. Visionary builds empire. Clean. Inspirational. Mostly fake.

Here's what actually happened to me: a company I poured years into restructured. I was out. Not dramatically. Not with a speech about following my dreams. Just... out.

And then I had to answer a question most people never sit with long enough: do I go find another seat at someone else's table, or do I build my own?

I chose to build. Not because I had some grand vision. Because I looked at my skill stack and realized something. The problems I solve, revenue operations architecture, GTM systems, pipeline engineering, these are expensive problems. Companies pay $200K+ salaries for people who can do this. Or they pay consultants.

The math was simple. I had the skills. I had the network. And for the first time, I had no golden handcuffs keeping me from testing whether I could do it solo.

Three months in, I can tell you: the hardest part isn't finding clients. It's unlearning the employee mindset. Waiting for permission. Wanting a roadmap. Needing someone to validate your priorities.

Nobody validates your priorities when you're solo. That's the terrifying part. It's also the point.
remixes.cta: If you've been laid off and you're considering going independent, ask yourself one question: do people already pay for the thing you're good at? If yes, you have more leverage than you think.

remixes.label: tactical-playbook-pivot
remixes.hook: I went from VP of Sales Ops to solo consultant in 90 days. Here's the actual playbook, not the inspirational version.
remixes.body:
I went from VP of Sales Ops to solo consultant in 90 days. Here's the actual playbook, not the inspirational version.

Week 1-2: Panic. Apply to a few jobs out of habit. Realize every JD feels like a downgrade from the problems I was solving.

Week 3-4: Audit. I listed every system I'd built, every pipeline I'd architected, every mess I'd cleaned up. Not resume bullet points. Actual deliverables with dollar values attached.

Week 5-8: Signal. I started posting what I know. Not thought leadership fluff. Actual breakdowns of GTM systems, revenue ops architecture, the stuff I'd been doing behind closed doors for years. Turns out people pay attention when you show your work.

Week 9-12: Pipeline. Three inbound conversations turned into two paid engagements. Not from job boards. From people who saw me explain something they were struggling with and thought, "I need that person."

The thing nobody tells you about career pivots: the skills transfer. The identity doesn't. You have to rebuild how you see yourself. That takes longer than finding clients.

I still catch myself thinking like an employee some mornings. Checking Slack that doesn't exist. Looking for a standup that isn't happening. The muscle memory of employment is strong.

But the muscle memory of building something yours is stronger.
remixes.cta: The 90-day window after a layoff is the most dangerous and most valuable time in your career. Don't waste it applying to jobs out of fear.

I didn't quit my job to start a company. I got laid off and decided not to go back.

Here's what I'd tell anyone in that same position: the narrative doesn't matter. The math does.

LinkedIn is full of clean founder stories. "I saw my calling." "I couldn't work for someone else anymore." These are retroactive narratives people build after the outcome is known.

The real decision framework is simpler. Ask three questions. First, do you have a skill stack that solves problems companies pay real money for? Second, do you have enough runway to survive 90 days without income? Third, can you tolerate the identity shift from "employee at Company X" to "person figuring it out"?

If you answer yes to the first two, the third one is just discomfort. Discomfort passes.

What I'd recommend: don't frame it as starting a company. Frame it as running an experiment. Give yourself 90 days to test whether your skills can generate revenue independently. If they can, you have a business. If they can't, you've lost nothing. You were going to job hunt anyway.

The people who struggle most with this transition aren't the ones lacking skills. They're the ones who need external validation to feel productive. Solo work strips that away completely. No manager telling you you're on track. No quarterly review confirming your value.

You have to build that internal compass yourself. That's the real pivot. Not the career change. The mindset change.
tones.provocateur:
I didn't quit my job to start a company. I got laid off and decided not to go back.

And honestly? Getting laid off was the best thing that could have happened. Because I never would have left voluntarily.

Let's be real about something. Most people in good corporate jobs are too comfortable to leave. The salary is fine. The benefits are fine. The work is... fine. And "fine" is the most dangerous word in your career. Fine means you'll ride it out for another 3 years, collect your RSUs, and wake up at 42 wondering what happened.

The layoff broke the spell. Suddenly I had to confront something uncomfortable: I'd been trading my best years building someone else's system. All those GTM architectures, pipeline engines, revenue models. Built on someone else's platform. Under someone else's name.

Here's the dirty secret about going independent. The skills you developed in corporate are worth more outside of it. Way more. Because inside a company, you're a cost center with a title. Outside, you're a solution with a price tag.

Every VP of Ops, every Director of RevOps, every senior IC reading this knows exactly what I mean. You've built systems worth millions. Your comp didn't reflect that. It never was going to.

The founder origin stories on LinkedIn are copium for people who want to believe the transition is clean. It's not. It's messy, scary, and the best professional decision I've made.

posts.title: AI infrastructure is the new cloud
posts.hook: In 2010, every startup needed AWS expertise. In 2026, every startup needs someone who can wire AI agents into their operations.
posts.body:
In 2010, every startup needed AWS expertise. In 2026, every startup needs someone who can wire AI agents into their operations.

The infrastructure layer for AI agents is still being built. And the companies building it are solving the exact same problems cloud providers solved 15 years ago:

... How do you scale reliably?
... How do you monitor what's running?
... How do you handle failures gracefully?
... How do you control costs?

Right now most teams are duct-taping agent infrastructure together. A cron here, a webhook there, API keys scattered across .env files, no observability, no cost tracking.

This is 2008-era cloud deployment. Manual, fragile, and expensive when it breaks.

The winners in this space will be the platforms that make agent deployment feel like Vercel made web deployment feel. Push your agent config, it runs, it scales, you can see what it's doing, and you get an alert when something goes wrong.

We're not there yet. Not close. But the demand is already massive. Every company I talk to wants to deploy agents. Almost none of them have the infrastructure to run them reliably.

That gap is a generational opportunity for whoever closes it first.
posts.cta: Watching this space closely. The agent infrastructure winners probably haven't launched yet.

inspired_by.author: Amanda Silverman
inspired_by.summary: Analyzes the AI infrastructure landscape, spotlighting startups building scalable agent hosting platforms. Discusses challenges in orchestration and monitoring for multi-agent systems. Recommends tools for developers to prototype automation at scale.

remixes.label: historical-parallel
remixes.hook: Every 15 years, a new infrastructure layer creates a gold rush. We're in the middle of one right now and most companies are still hiring for the last one.
remixes.body:
Every 15 years, a new infrastructure layer creates a gold rush. We're in the middle of one right now and most companies are still hiring for the last one.

2000: Web hosting. Companies needed someone who could keep servers running. Entire consultancies were built around "we'll manage your Linux boxes."

2010: Cloud migration. AWS, GCP, Azure. The people who understood auto-scaling, containerization, and cloud-native architecture became the most sought-after hires in tech.

2026: Agent orchestration. The companies that figure out how to deploy, monitor, and scale AI agents inside their operations will win their markets. The ones that don't will hire consultants from the ones that did.

The pattern is the same every time. New infrastructure layer emerges. Early adopters build custom solutions. A tooling ecosystem develops. Winners consolidate.

Right now we're between stages two and three. Custom solutions everywhere. Duct tape and API calls. Every company wiring agents differently. No standards. No consensus on monitoring. No agreed-upon way to handle agent failures.

This is exactly where cloud was in 2008. Before CloudFormation. Before Terraform. Before anyone agreed on how infrastructure-as-code should work.

The people building agent infrastructure right now, the orchestration layers, the observability tools, the failure recovery systems, they're building the next AWS. Most of them don't know it yet.

And the people learning to operate these systems? They're the cloud engineers of 2026. The job title doesn't exist on most org charts yet. It will.
remixes.cta: The highest-leverage skill you can build right now isn't prompt engineering. It's agent operations. The infrastructure layer is where the durable careers get built.

remixes.label: buyer-pain-angle
remixes.hook: I talked to 12 ops leaders last month. Every single one had the same problem: they bought AI tools but can't wire them together.
remixes.body:
I talked to 12 ops leaders last month. Every single one had the same problem: they bought AI tools but can't wire them together.

The AI adoption curve has hit an ugly middle phase. Companies have budget. They have licenses. They have a Notion doc full of "AI use cases" from some offsite. What they don't have is anyone who can actually build the connective tissue.

Here's what I keep seeing. A marketing team using ChatGPT for copy. A sales team testing an AI SDR tool. A CS team piloting an AI ticket classifier. None of them talking to each other. No shared data layer. No unified monitoring. No one thinking about how these pieces compose into a system.

This is the cloud migration playbook all over again. In 2012, companies had servers in three places. On-prem, colo, and "someone put a staging environment on AWS." The companies that won were the ones who hired infrastructure people to unify it.

We're at the same inflection point. The winners won't be the companies with the most AI tools. They'll be the ones who build the orchestration layer that makes those tools work together.

The role that solves this doesn't have a clean title yet. It's part systems architect, part ops engineer, part AI wrangler. Some people are calling it AI Operations. Others call it Agent Infrastructure.

Whatever you call it, it's the most in-demand skill set nobody's actively training for.
remixes.cta: If your AI tools are siloed across teams with no shared infrastructure, you don't have an AI strategy. You have a collection of experiments.

In 2010, every startup needed AWS expertise. In 2026, every startup needs someone who can wire AI agents into their operations.

The infrastructure layer for AI agents is still being built. And the companies building it are solving the exact same problems cloud providers solved 15 years ago:

... How do you scale reliably?
... How do you monitor what's running?
... How do you handle failures gracefully?
... How do you control costs?

Right now most teams are duct-taping agent infrastructure together. A cron here, a webhook there, API keys scattered across .env files, no observability, no cost tracking.

This is 2008-era cloud deployment. Manual, fragile, and expensive when it breaks.

The winners in this space will be the platforms that make agent deployment feel like Vercel made web deployment feel. Push your agent config, it runs, it scales, you can see what it's doing, and you get an alert when something goes wrong.

We're not there yet. Not close. But the demand is already massive. Every company I talk to wants to deploy agents. Almost none of them have the infrastructure to run them reliably.

That gap is a generational opportunity for whoever closes it first.

posts.title: content strategy for pipeline not vanity
posts.hook: Your content isn't a brand exercise. It's the top of your outbound funnel and most teams treat it like an afterthought.
posts.body:
Your content isn't a brand exercise. It's the top of your outbound funnel and most teams treat it like an afterthought.

I've been running an experiment for months. Every piece of content I publish feeds directly into my GTM pipeline.

A LinkedIn post about Clay workflows? I track who engages. Those engagers get enriched, scored, and routed into targeted sequences. Not generic "hey saw you liked my post" messages. Sequences built around the specific problem that post addressed.

A blog post about AI in GTM? The organic traffic gets cookied, matched to accounts in my CRM, and prioritized for outbound if they fit the ICP.

A Reddit comment sharing a real workflow? That drives traffic to a landing page with a specific conversion path.

This isn't content marketing. It's content-led demand generation. The difference is every piece has a measurable path to pipeline.

The tactical setup:
✅ PostHog tracking on all content properties
✅ Account-level matching in Attio CRM
✅ Engagement-based scoring in Clay
✅ Automated routing into relevant sequences

Most GTM teams have content people and outbound people in separate rooms. The teams generating the most pipeline per dollar have those functions wired together at the data layer.
posts.cta: If your content team and outbound team don't share a dashboard, you're leaving pipeline on the table.

inspired_by.author: Kyle Coleman
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a content strategy playbook for revenue ops teams to support outbound automation at scale. Details how to engineer GTM motions with personalized sequences driven by sales ops data. Includes case study on 3x pipeline growth.

remixes.label: reverse-funnel-breakdown
remixes.hook: I reverse-engineered my last 5 closed deals. Every single one started with a LinkedIn post.
remixes.body:
I reverse-engineered my last 5 closed deals. Every single one started with a LinkedIn post.

Not a viral post. Not something with thousands of impressions. Just a specific breakdown of how I solve a specific problem. Someone in my ICP read it, thought "this person gets my problem," and reached out.

That's when I realized content isn't a brand play. It's the most efficient top-of-funnel motion for solo operators and small teams.

Here's how the pipeline actually works. I publish a post about a real GTM problem. I track engagement in PostHog. The people who engage, like, comment, share, they get enriched through my data pipeline. Company, role, tech stack, revenue stage. That enrichment feeds a scoring model.

High-scoring engagers get a personalized sequence. Not "hey I saw you liked my post." Something specific to their company and the problem they're likely facing based on the content they engaged with.

Low-scoring engagers get added to a nurture track. More content. More signal collection. Wait for the score to climb.

The conversion rate on this pipeline is 3x my cold outbound. Because by the time I reach out, they already trust that I understand their problem. The content did the credibility work before the first email.

Most content strategies fail because they optimize for reach instead of pipeline. Vanity metrics feel good. Revenue feels better.

Every post I write has a hypothesis attached: "This topic will attract [persona] with [problem]." If the engagement data doesn't match the hypothesis, I adjust. If it does, I double down.
remixes.cta: Track your content-to-pipeline path for 30 days. You'll find that 80% of your posts generate zero pipeline and 20% generate almost all of it. Double down on the 20%.

remixes.label: anti-brand-rant
remixes.hook: Hot take: most B2B content strategies are an expensive way to feel productive while generating zero pipeline.
remixes.body:
Hot take: most B2B content strategies are an expensive way to feel productive while generating zero pipeline.

I've audited content programs at a dozen companies. Same pattern every time. Content team publishes 3-4 posts per week. Marketing reports on impressions and engagement rate. Sales ignores all of it. Pipeline doesn't move.

The disconnect is structural. Content teams are measured on content metrics. Impressions. Engagement. Follower growth. Sales teams are measured on pipeline and revenue. There's no shared metric between them.

So content optimizes for what gets likes and sales does cold outbound like the content doesn't exist. Two parallel operations, zero integration.

Here's what integration actually looks like. Every piece of content has a target persona and target problem. Engagement data flows into your enrichment pipeline. Enriched engagers get scored. High-score leads get routed to sales with context: "This person engaged with your post about pipeline orchestration. They're a VP of Rev Ops at a Series B company. Here's their likely tech stack."

Now sales has a warm lead with context instead of a cold name with a phone number.

The technology to build this exists today. CRM APIs. Enrichment tools. Scoring models. Sequence builders. The problem isn't technical. It's organizational. Content and outbound sit in different departments with different leaders and different KPIs.

The companies that merge these functions, even partially, see pipeline numbers that make their competitors confused.
remixes.cta: If your content team can't tell you which posts generated pipeline last quarter, your content strategy is a hobby. Make it a revenue function.

Let me walk you through how to build a content-to-pipeline system that actually generates revenue.

I've been running this for months and the results are clear. Every piece of content I publish feeds directly into my GTM pipeline through a structured workflow.

Step one: publish content about specific problems your ICP faces. Not thought leadership. Not trends. Specific, technical breakdowns of problems and solutions.

Step two: track engagement at the person level. Who liked it. Who commented. Who shared. This data is available through platform APIs and tools like PostHog.

Step three: enrich those engagers. Company, role, revenue stage, tech stack. Build a profile that tells you whether this person has the problem you solve and the budget to pay for a solution.

Step four: score and route. High-fit engagers go into personalized outbound sequences. The message references the specific content they engaged with and the specific problem it addressed. Low-fit engagers go into a nurture track.

The key insight: by the time you reach out, credibility is already established. The content did the trust-building work. Your outbound email isn't cold anymore.

I'd recommend starting small. Pick your three best-performing posts from the last quarter. Manually enrich the top engagers. Run a targeted sequence against them. Measure the response rate against your standard cold outbound.

Most teams see a 2-3x improvement. That's your proof of concept for building the automated version.

A LinkedIn post about Clay workflows? I track who engages. Those engagers get enriched and scored. High scores get a targeted sequence. Not generic "hey saw you liked my post" garbage. Specific, contextual outreach that converts at 3x my cold outbound rate.

Vanity metrics are a drug. Pipeline is the cure.

posts.title: scaling laws for solo builders
posts.hook: The scaling laws for AI models have a parallel nobody's discussing: scaling laws for solo builders.
posts.body:
The scaling laws for AI models have a parallel nobody's discussing: scaling laws for solo builders.

In ML, there's a predictable relationship between compute, data, and model performance. Throw more resources at training, get better outputs. Karpathy and others have mapped this precisely.

Solo builders have their own scaling law now. And AI tools broke the old curve.

Old curve: one person can maintain roughly 2-3 production systems before quality degrades. Context switching kills you. Bugs pile up. You spend more time firefighting than building.

New curve: one person with AI tools, good architecture, and automation can maintain 10+ production systems. I'm running proof of that right now. Three websites, a CRM pipeline, daily content crons, a chat system, monitoring dashboards, and an outbound engine. All from one machine.

The variable that changed isn't intelligence or work ethic. It's the cost of context switching. When your AI collaborator can reload an entire project's context in seconds, switching between systems stops being expensive.

But there's a ceiling most people hit: architecture quality. If your systems are poorly structured, AI tools amplify the mess. If they're clean, AI tools amplify the velocity.

The new scaling law for builders: your output scales with the quality of your systems, not the hours in your day.
posts.cta: The builders who invest in clean architecture now are going to have an absurd advantage in 12 months. The compound returns on good structure are just starting.

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Explores advancements in AI infrastructure for training large models, focusing on efficient scaling laws and custom hardware like TPUs. Critiques current bottlenecks in data pipelines and proposes open-source tools for developer accessibility. Predicts a shift towards agent-optimized infra stacks.

remixes.label: personal-output-log
remixes.hook: Last Tuesday I shipped a complete website, wrote 4 blog posts, built a data pipeline, and handled two client calls. I'm one person.
remixes.body:
Last Tuesday I shipped a complete website, wrote 4 blog posts, built a data pipeline, and handled two client calls. I'm one person.

Two years ago that would have been a team's weekly output. Today it's a Tuesday. And I'm not working harder. I'm working with better tools.

Here's what changed. I stopped thinking about AI as a productivity hack and started thinking about it as infrastructure. The difference matters.

A productivity hack is "use ChatGPT to write emails faster." Infrastructure is a system of crons, agents, and pipelines that execute tasks autonomously while you focus on the work that actually requires your brain.

My stack runs daily crons that generate blog content, sync data across platforms, score leads, and monitor my sites. I built these systems once. They run every day without intervention.

The scaling law for solo builders looks like this. Output used to scale linearly with hours worked. More hours, more output. AI tools broke that curve. Now output scales with system quality. Better systems, dramatically more output, same hours.

But here's . The curve only breaks if you invest in architecture. If you're using AI as a copilot for each individual task, you get a 2x improvement. If you build AI into your operational infrastructure, you get 10x.

The 10x isn't from the AI being smart. It's from the compounding effect of automated systems running 24/7. Each system I build adds to total output capacity permanently. That's the scaling law. Not linear improvement. Compound improvement.
remixes.cta: The question isn't whether you're using AI. It's whether you're using it as a tool or as infrastructure. The gap between those two approaches gets wider every month.

remixes.label: karpathy-framework
remixes.hook: Karpathy mapped scaling laws for neural networks. Nobody's mapping them for one-person companies. So I started tracking.
remixes.body:
Karpathy mapped scaling laws for neural networks. Nobody's mapping them for one-person companies. So I started tracking.

In ML, scaling laws describe a predictable relationship. More compute and more data yield better model performance. The curves are logarithmic but reliable. You can predict performance before training.

I've been tracking my own output curves for three months. Hours worked. Tasks completed. Revenue generated. Systems built. And the data shows something interesting.

Pre-AI tools: my output scaled roughly 1:1 with time invested. 8 hours of work produced about 8 hours of output. Standard.

With AI copilot usage: about 1.5-2x. Write code faster, draft content faster, research faster. Nice but not transformative.

With AI infrastructure (crons, agents, pipelines): the ratio broke. Some weeks I'm producing what looks like 5-8x my pre-AI output. Not because any single task is faster. Because systems are running in parallel, producing output while I sleep.

The key variable isn't AI capability. It's architecture quality. A sloppy automation breaks every third run and costs you time debugging. A clean system runs for months and compounds.

This maps exactly to neural network scaling laws. In ML, data quality matters more than data quantity at scale. For solo builders, system quality matters more than tool quantity.

I've talked to other solo builders seeing the same curves. The ones who invested in clean architecture early are pulling away. The ones still using AI as a per-task copilot are improving linearly. Same tools. Different infrastructure. Wildly different output.

The divergence is only going to accelerate.
remixes.cta: Track your own output curve for 30 days. Hours in vs. deliverables out. Then ask: am I scaling linearly or exponentially? The answer tells you whether you're using AI as a tool or as infrastructure.

posts.title: open source your process not your product
posts.hook: I give away every prompt, every workflow, every scoring rubric I build. People ask why I'm not worried about competition.
posts.body:
I give away every prompt, every workflow, every scoring rubric I build. People ask why I'm not worried about competition.

Because the process is not the moat. The system is.

I can hand you my exact Clay enrichment waterfall. Column by column. Every API call, every scoring formula, every AI prompt. You'll have the blueprint.

You still won't have:
... the crons that run it daily without intervention
... the feedback loops that improve targeting every week
... the CRM integrations that route signals to the right sequences
... the months of iteration that tuned every threshold

A recipe is not a restaurant.

Addy Osmani open-sourced tools used by millions of developers. It made him more credible, not less competitive. The people who actually implement what you share become your best referral network.

Every time I drop a full workflow in the comments, three things happen. Someone tries it and gets stuck, then asks for help. Someone tries it and succeeds, then tells people where they learned it. Someone reads it and realizes they need someone to build this for them.

All three outcomes are good for business.

The builders hoarding their processes are protecting something that depreciates monthly. The ones sharing are building something that compounds.
posts.cta: No gatekeeping. Drop your best workflow in the comments. The people who matter will remember where they found it.

inspired_by.author: Addy Osmani
inspired_by.summary: Addy describes his career pivot from engineering to leadership while side-building open-source tools and sharing the process publicly. He offers advice on upskilling, networking on LinkedIn, and balancing full-time work with personal projects. It's a realistic guide for tech career changers.

remixes.label: moat-misconception
remixes.hook: People think sharing your process gives competitors an advantage. It doesn't. It gives you one.
remixes.body:
People think sharing your process gives competitors an advantage. It doesn't. It gives you one.

I've been publishing my exact GTM workflows for months. Clay enrichment waterfalls. Scoring rubrics. AI prompts. Every column, every API call, every formula. All public.

Here's what happened. My pipeline got stronger, not weaker.

Because sharing your process does three things that hoarding never will.

First, it builds trust at scale. When someone sees your actual workflow, they don't think "now I can do it myself." They think "this person clearly knows what they're doing." The blueprint is a proof of competence. Most people who see it won't build it. They'll hire you to build it for them.

Second, it attracts inbound. Every workflow I publish is a magnet for people who have the same problem but not the time or skill to solve it themselves. They find the post, try to implement it, realize the system around it is what makes it work, and reach out.

Third, it compounds your reputation. In a world where everyone is gatekeeping their "secret sauce," being transparent is a differentiator. People remember who taught them something useful. And they come back when they need help.

The actual moat was never the process. Processes are replicable. The moat is the system that runs the process. The crons. The monitoring. The error handling. The institutional knowledge of what breaks and how to fix it. The years of iteration baked into the infrastructure.

You can't copy a system by reading a LinkedIn post. You can only build one yourself. And most people won't.
remixes.cta: Share your best work publicly. The people who can copy it were never your customers. The people who can't will become them.

remixes.label: gatekeeping-is-fear
remixes.hook: Every time I see "DM me for my framework" on LinkedIn, I see someone who doesn't trust their own competitive advantage.
remixes.body:
Every time I see "DM me for my framework" on LinkedIn, I see someone who doesn't trust their own competitive advantage.

Let me be direct about this. If your entire business can be replicated by someone reading a LinkedIn post, you don't have a business. You have a trick. And tricks have a short shelf life.

I publish everything. Prompts. Workflows. Scoring models. Technical architecture. People have my exact Clay enrichment waterfall. They know my scoring rubric. They've seen my AI prompts.

None of them are my competitors. Here's why.

A workflow without a system is a recipe without a kitchen. You can read my enrichment waterfall and understand every step. But can you run it daily at scale? Can you handle the API failures that happen every third run? Can you adapt it when a data provider changes their schema? Can you integrate the output into six downstream systems?

The execution layer is the moat. Always has been. The playbook is the table stakes.

Gatekeeping is a tax on trust. Every framework you hide behind a DM wall is a missed opportunity to prove you know what you're doing. The people who would pay you were never going to steal your workflow. They were going to see it and think, "I want this person building my system."

The best business development I've ever done is giving away my best work for free. Full stop. Not "free sample to hook them." Actually free. No email gate. No DM required.

The clients who found me through open content are my best clients. They arrived pre-sold on my approach. No convincing needed.
remixes.cta: Drop your best workflow in the comments. Don't gate it. Don't tease it. Just share it. The right people will remember where they found it.

tones.builder:
I give away every prompt, every workflow, every scoring rubric I build. People ask why I'm not worried about competition.

Because the process is not the moat. The system is.

I can hand you my exact Clay enrichment waterfall. Column by column. Every API call, every scoring formula, every AI prompt. You'll have the blueprint.

You still won't have:
... the crons that run it daily without intervention
... the feedback loops that improve targeting every week
... the CRM integrations that route signals to the right sequences
... the months of iteration that tuned every threshold

A recipe is not a restaurant.

Addy Osmani open-sourced tools used by millions of developers. It made him more credible, not less competitive. The people who actually implement what you share become your best referral network.

Every time I drop a full workflow in the comments, three things happen. Someone tries it and gets stuck, then asks for help. Someone tries it and succeeds, then tells people where they learned it. Someone reads it and realizes they need someone to build this for them.

All three outcomes are good for business.

The builders hoarding their processes are protecting something that depreciates monthly. The ones sharing are building something that compounds.
tones.advisor:
I give away every prompt, every workflow, every scoring rubric I build. People ask why I'm not worried about competition.

Because the process is not the moat. The system is.

Here's the framework I'd recommend for deciding what to share publicly and why.

I can hand you my exact Clay enrichment waterfall. Column by column. Every API call, every scoring formula, every AI prompt. You'll have the blueprint.

You still won't have the crons that run it daily without intervention. The error handling that recovers from API failures at 2 AM. The monitoring that alerts when data quality degrades. The iteration history, knowing which approaches failed and why, that shaped every decision in the current system.

This is the key insight: processes are replicable. Systems are not. A process is a set of steps. A system is a process plus infrastructure plus operational knowledge plus continuous iteration. The gap between them is where your competitive advantage lives.

My recommendation: share your processes openly and invest your energy into building systems that are hard to replicate. The sharing itself becomes a competitive advantage because it builds trust, attracts inbound, and positions you as the expert in your space.

The math works in your favor. For every 100 people who see your workflow, maybe 5 will try to build it. Of those 5, maybe 1 will get it running. And that person was never going to be your client anyway. They were going to build it regardless. You just earned goodwill with the other 99.

No gatekeeping. Share your best work. The people who matter will remember where they found it.
tones.provocateur:
I give away every prompt, every workflow, every scoring rubric I build. People ask why I'm not worried about competition.

Because the "DM me for my framework" crowd is playing the wrong game entirely.

Let me be blunt. If someone can read your LinkedIn post, replicate your entire operation, and take your clients, you never had a real business. You had a party trick. And party tricks don't scale.

I can hand you my exact Clay enrichment waterfall. Column by column. Every API call, every scoring formula, every AI prompt. Here. Take it.

Now build the crons that run it at midnight without failing. Build the error recovery that handles the three different ways the enrichment API breaks every week. Build the monitoring layer that catches data quality drift before it corrupts your scoring model. Build the six downstream integrations that consume the output.

Oh, and do it while also running a business, serving clients, and creating content.

You won't. Not because you're not smart enough. Because building a system takes months of compounding iteration that can't be shortcut by reading a post.

Gatekeeping is insecurity wearing a business strategy costume. Every "DM me" is a person who doesn't trust that their actual expertise is the differentiator. They think the workflow is the moat. It's not. The system is the moat. The institutional knowledge is the moat. The years of compounding improvement are the moat.

The most successful business development I've done this year is publishing my best work completely free. No gate. No funnel. No "exclusive framework." Just the work.

Clients arrive pre-sold. No pitch deck needed.
